query: DynamoDB analytics limitations PartiQL complex queries real-time analytics 2024
answer: DynamoDB has limitations for complex queries and real-time analytics; it excels for simple key-value access but struggles with intricate data models. Use cases often involve real-time data processing with tools like Tinybird. In 2024, focus was on better integrations and scalable applications.

results.title: Enhancing DynamoDB for flexible querying and real-time analytics
results.snippet: DynamoDB is a distributed NoSQL that's laser-focused on solving a specific problem. For classic OLTP workloads with predictable access

results.title: Top Use Cases for DynamoDB in 2024 - Tinybird
results.snippet: Amazon's DynamoDB is a fast, NoSQL key-value database widely adopted by engineering teams who need a real-time, distributed data storage solution within the AWS ecosystem. DynamoDB works for banking because it can handle the global scale and the fast read/write patterns needed to keep financial information up to date both at the server and in the user-facing application, and because it offers strongly consistent reads even on a distributed architecture, both banks and customers have assurance that their financial data is accurate and up to date. In addition to your preferred billing option, you'll also pay for things like data storage (including backups), data transfer costs (either to external clouds/services or different AWS regions), replication to global tables, the number of DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) nodes you have provisioned, read/write operations for DyanmoDB Streams, and any reserved capacity you want to maintain for long term workloads. Tinybird uses DynamoDB Streams to maintain an up-to-date replica of your DynamoDB tables within a highly available columnar database built specifically for real-time, user-facing analytics.

results.title: Relational DynamoDB: When DynamoDB Stops Being Simple
results.snippet: # Relational DynamoDB: When DynamoDB Stops Being Simple. Let’s be clear about something upfront: if you’re using DynamoDB as a simple key-value store—storing session data, caching API responses, or managing user preferences—you don’t need any of this. But the moment you try to use DynamoDB as your primary database with a real data model—with relationships between entities, complex access patterns, and business logic—everything changes. Suddenly, you’re fighting the database at every turn. This is where teams either give up on DynamoDB or level up their approach with single table design and proper tooling. // Multiple access patterns, derived keys, complex queries // Multiple access patterns, derived keys, complex queries // Multiple access patterns, derived keys, complex queries // This is where teams either adopt proper patterns or suffer // This is where teams either adopt proper patterns or suffer // This is where teams either adopt proper patterns or suffer   // Need orders by date across all users? You can’t just “figure out the queries later.” Instead, you must know every single access pattern upfront and design your data specifically to support those patterns.
